learning_outcomes.description: Understand and apply basic statistical tests in applied linguistics and second language acquisition.
Understand the role of quantitative evidence in describing and explaining language learning and use.
Read quantitative research in these areas in a more critical and productive manner.
Use statistical software to analyse and present data.
Write up results for a dissertation or publication.

assessments.task_description:
The R & Statistics Learning Journal is a reflective assessment designed to support your ongoing engagement with course content and encourage the gradual development of your understanding of R programming, statistical concepts, and data analysis practices.

Across the semester, you are required to submit five short reflective journal entries, each based on a selected week of the course. Each entry should be between 100 and 200 words in length and is worth 4% of your final grade, contributing a total of 20%.

Purpose

The purpose of this assessment is to promote regular reflection on your learning and to support you in connecting R programming and statistical theory to practical application. Through these reflections, you will consolidate your understanding of statistical concepts, identify areas where you need further development, and consider how different analytical techniques can be applied to real linguistic data.

What Weeks Can I Write About?

You may write learning journal entries on any weeks from WEEK 3 to WEEK 12. You choose which five weeks to reflect on—they do not need to be consecutive. Important: If you submit more than 5 entries, only the first 5 submitted will be marked.

Deadlines and Submission

Each journal entry must be submitted by Monday 1:00 PM of the week following the week it reflects on.

Deadline Examples

  • Week 3 content → Entry due Monday of Week 4 at 1:00 PM
  • Week 9 content → Entry due Monday of Week 10 at 1:00 PM

What Should My Entry Contain?

Journal entries should reflect on your learning from a particular week and engage meaningfully with the content covered. Entries should be specific, detailed, and substantive—not superficial or overly general. Content will vary depending on the week:

For weeks covering statistical concepts (e.g., descriptive statistics, inferential statistics):

  • What statistical concept did you learn?
  • Why is it important for linguistic research?
  • What aspects did you find challenging or particularly insightful?

For weeks covering R programming (e.g., basic R syntax, data processing):

  • What R functions or techniques did you learn?
  • Can you explain what a piece of code does in your own words?
  • Where might you apply this in your own data analysis?

For weeks covering application (e.g., visualization, regression analysis):

  • How did you apply the statistical or R technique to data?
  • What did the results reveal, and why does it matter?
  • What limitations or questions emerged from your analysis?

Avoid generic, superficial entries that lack specific examples or fail to go beyond description. Good entries demonstrate genuine engagement with course concepts and show how you're building your understanding.

Word Count Requirement

Your entry must be between 100 and 200 words. This is a strict requirement.

Use a word counter to check your entry before submitting. Every word counts!

How to Submit

  • Submit via Blackboard in the designated Learning Journal submission box
  • Include your name and student ID at the top of your document (does not feed into the count for the word count limit)

See example entries on Blackboard to understand what strong reflection looks like.

assessments.task_description:
The R Code Interview is a 10-minute live, face-to-face assessment designed to evaluate your genuine understanding of R programming and your ability to work with code. Rather than writing code from scratch, you will be asked to explain existing R code, identify errors, and describe how to fix them. This assessment tests your comprehension of R syntax, logic, and data manipulation—essential skills for statistical analysis.

This assessment is worth 30% of your final grade.

Purpose

The purpose of this assessment is to verify that you have genuine, independent understanding of R programming and statistical concepts. The interview is AI-secure and identity-verified, ensuring authentic assessment of your knowledge.

What You Need to Know

The Five Code Scripts

Five R scripts will be made available to you on Blackboard by Week 9. Each script:

  • Loads a linguistic dataset
  • Processes the data (cleaning, filtering, transforming)
  • Generates summary statistics
  • Creates a data visualization

Each script contains intentional errors, such as:

  • Missing brackets or parentheses
  • Missing colons or operators
  • Incorrect file paths
  • Missing assignment symbols
  • Logic errors (wrong function, incorrect argument)
  • Syntax errors

You are expected to study all five scripts before your interview. They are practice material to help you prepare.

Interview Timing

A sign-up sheet will be posted on Blackboard during Week 8

  • Interviews will be held in WEEKS 10 and 11 outside of your regular class time.
  • Each interview is 10 minutes long.

What Happens During the Interview

The interview follows this structure:

  • The tutor will display an R code script on screen
  • You will be asked to explain what sections of the code do
  • The tutor will highlight or point to specific lines and ask: "What does this do?"
  • You will be asked to identify errors in the code and explain what needs to change.
  • The tutor may point out any errors you have missed and ask you to explain them.

This will happen multiple times during your 10-minute interview with different code sections.

Important Details

Identity Verification

You must bring a valid student ID or other official identification to verify your identity at the start of the interview.

What You Can and Cannot Bring

  • You CANNOT bring any materials into the interview (notes, summaries, cheat sheets, books)
  • You CANNOT use any electronic devices (laptops, phones, tablets)
  • You can access the code scripts shown by the tutor during the interview

Recording

Your interview will be recorded for documentation purposes. This recording is used for quality assurance and record-keeping.

How to Prepare

  • Study all five R scripts provided on Blackboard
  • Line-by-line: Read and understand what each line of code does
  • Error-hunt: Try to identify the errors in each script
  • Explanation: Practice explaining what the error is and why it's wrong
  • Solutions: Think about how you would fix each error
  • Review course materials: Refer back to tutorials and lectures on R syntax and data processing

A student study guide with example error explanations will be available on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The R Code Interview is a 10-minute live, face-to-face assessment.

assessments.task_description:
The Statistical Analysis Report assessment serves as a practical application of the knowledge and skills acquired throughout the course. It offers an opportunity to engage with a real-world, language- or culture-related dataset of your choice, honing your abilities to describe, summarise, visualise, and quantitatively analyse data. By undertaking this assessment, you will gain insights into the approaches and practices employed by data analysts in the field of linguistics and applied linguistics.

This assessment is worth 50% of your final grade.

Report Requirements

Word Count

Your report must be 2000 words, excluding R code, R output, and references.

Required Sections

Your report should include the following key sections:

  • 1. Motivation and Research Question: Begin by providing a clear rationale for your research question, explaining its significance and relevance to the field of study.
  • 2. Hypothesis Formulation: Develop a hypothesis that corresponds to your research question, demonstrating a hypothesis-driven approach to your analysis.
  • 3. Data Description, Tabulation, and Visualisation: Thoroughly describe the dataset utilised in your analysis, employing appropriate tables, figures, and visualisations to present the data in a comprehensive manner.
  • 4. Statistical Analysis Description: Describe the statistical techniques and methods employed to analyse the data. Provide a detailed explanation of the chosen approaches, justifying their relevance to the research question.
  • 5. Results Reporting: Present the results of your analysis using a combination of prose, tables, and figures. Ensure that your findings are clear, concise, and effectively support your hypothesis.
  • 6. Critical Evaluation: Conduct a critical evaluation of your analysis, highlighting any potential issues or limitations that may impact the validity or generalisability of your results. Offer thoughtful insights into how these shortcomings could be addressed or mitigated.
  • 7. References: Include a reference section following APA7 format, acknowledging any external sources used in your report.

R Code and Output

It is crucial that you also include the R code you use in your analysis. R code and R output should be included in an appendix (not counted toward the 2000-word limit). The code should be well-commented, explaining the procedures you implemented. This demonstrates your practical mastery of R and allows others to verify and replicate your analysis.

Dataset Selection

You must select a dataset related to language or culture. The dataset can be one you created yourself, sourced from online repositories, downloaded for the resources for this course, or obtained from academic studies. Your choice should be motivated by your research question and the analytical techniques taught in this course.

Submission

  • Submit via Blackboard by the due date specified in the course schedule
  • Submit as a single PDF or Word document
  • Include your name and student ID at the top of your document

By completing this Statistical Analysis Report, you will demonstrate your proficiency in data analysis, hypothesis formulation, result interpretation, and critical evaluation. This assessment provides a valuable opportunity to apply your knowledge in a practical context and gain hands-on experience in the field of data analysis.
